FILEDESC.SOURCEDESC: Created from original research by members of the Orlando Project

AUTHORSUMMARY.P: ID is a Pakistani-Scottish poet, artist, and filmmaker, and a significant voice in the representation of the South Asian diaspora in poetry. She has published seven collections of poetry, each featuring her own illustrations throughout the pages. Her work often explores her liminal or outsider status and reflects on socio-political subjects such as displacement and migration, gender, Islamophobia, religious fundamentalism, the uncertainty, loneliness, and anonymity of exile (Das) and articulates challenges faced by marginalized communities as they navigate cultural difference and social structures. Her style is simple and sensuous, her feeling strongly rooted in the present moment.

CHRONSTRUCT.CHRONPROSE: ID was born in Lahore, Pakistan.

SHORTPROSE.P: She has one older sister.

ID’s family emigrated from Pakistan to Scotland when she was six months old, and first settled in a house at the edge of img: only one settlement tag per place (unless it needs repeating in nesting smaller element, like Glasgow here.The Gorbals in Glasgow BBC The Conversation . As her father’s business became more successful, her family moved to Pollokshields, a more affluent suburb of Glasgow, intoa lovely house with a beautiful garden.. BBC Desert Island Discs

ID has described growing up in a Muslim Calvinist household,
She has used this phrase more than once to convey the strictness of her family's Islamic observance to an audience more familiar with Christian sects. loving but conservative and sheltered. Luck is the Hookimg: insufficient bibcit: needs page. The outside world was a no-man’s land, except for school, which was a safe haven. Olney Interview, York Notes As a teenager, Imtiaz was given the educational freedom to write and experiment with art, and there was enough money for her to enrol in dancing classes. However as a girl growing up in a conservative Muslim household, she was not permitted to go out dancing or partake in the 1960s club scene in Glasgow. A lot of my time in Glasgow was spent at my window looking out over the city and the shining lights and feeling as if all of life was happening somewhere else, somewhere out there, somewhere where I was not.
Desert Island Discs Although her household was conservative, she was never required to be in purdah, or veiled, herself. It was on a trip to Pakistan as a teenager that she first realized that as a Muslim female, she was there as someone's daughter, or someone's sister, and later she would be there as somene's wife. Haider 247
Her father's success in business meant a shift upward in the family's middle-class status, from marginal to well-established, marked by their change in address.

Reflecting on her Asian origin and her upbringing in Scotland, ID has said, there was one country inside the house and one outside, not necessarily in a bad way. I could slip from one to the other, the way children slip from one language to another if they are bilingual. Interview, Shivdasani 129
Scotland is in my blood. Like my father did, I love the sound and feel of the language on my tongue. All the years in Bombay stole my accent but I get it back if I spend a few days in Scotland. Scotsman Yet, visiting Pakistan for the first time at the age of thirteen, Imtiaz found it to be a landscape where she was completely at home. Shamsie 301 The author biography that appears in her poetry volumes characterizes hers as a life of transitions: childhood, exile, journeying, home, displacement, religious strife and terror, and latterly, grief. Luck is the Hookimg: add page-equivalent, either prelims, or back page, or cover. ID has reflected that I knew I didn’t belong in one place but I began to think strongly that this wasn’t a bad thing. . . . My real culture and country is movement, transition, crossing over.
Shamsie 301 ID’s parents embraced life in Scotland, especially her father, who enjoyed being the centre of their social circle.

Little is known about the background of IDs mother, except that her maiden name was Mobarak and she was a homemaker, an adept seamstress and an excellent cook. She had learned enough English to communicate effectively, but was always more comfortable speaking Punjabi. Family tradition recalled her making a smocked dress for Imtiaz's sister to wear when Queen Elizabeth II visited Glasgow after her coronation.img: problem here. Queen's visit to Glasgow was before date we give for ID's birth. BMakes birth date highly questionable, tho it's repeated in every source I've seen, not apparently dependent on each other. What's up? Have rephrased to cover possible shakiness of story. BBC Reflections on Majesty . Her feasts were, according to Imtiaz, legend. Reflections on Majesty
After ID's elopement around 1971, she never saw her mother again.

Choudhury Shair was a self-made businessman who arrived in London to study, but changed direction after visiting friends in Glasgow who were door-to-door salesmen of pinnies and nylons. Desert Island Discs Reflections on Majesty He found that he loved Scotland, and decided to stay. Soon he was importing goods from factories he had set up in Punjab. BBC Desert Island Discs When Imtiaz was still a baby, he purchased one of the first televisions among their neighbours, and was delighted to be able to invite everyone over to watch the 1953 coronation. BBC Desert Island Discs ID has stated that her father “was always very pleased with fitting in with local habits and customs but he was especially happy and proud to be able to speak like a proper ‘Wegian.” The Scotsman When she eloped with a Hindu Indian man, her family disowned her. After she had not seen her father for ten years, her sister let her know he would be flying into Heathrow Airport. ID went to the arrivals gate and approached him. I saw him coming though and went up to him. I said hello and he said hello and kept walking as if I were an acquaintance. I said I’m Imtiaz, I’m your daughter, and he didn’t recognize me. I had green hair at that point, with spikes. He carried on walking though very politely. He said, I‘m sorry, I didn’t realize, gave me a hug, and went on into the domestic transit. BBC Desert Island Discs
Choudhury Shair died at ninety-nine years old. BBC Desert Island Discs

ID has at least one sister, Shenaz, who is older than her. She describes from family tradition (she was too young to remember it) her mother wrapping Shenaz's hair in rags so that she would have ringlets to go with her hand-smocked dress for Queen Elizabeth II's coronation visit to Glasgow. National Library of Scotland BBC Desert Island Discs

Imtiaz Shiar attended Hutchesons' Grammar School, an upmarket provate school in Glasgow where she was the first South Asian girl to attend. BBC Desert Island Discs
In school I was considered a freak because I was the only Pakistani girl there. I've valued that separation and difference. I wanted to be different. De Souza 114 Bowell

ID went on from Hutchey's to the University of Glasgow. She received her Master of Arts (which in Scotland is the first degree awarded) in English Literature and Philosophy. Prabook, https://prabook.com/web/imtiaz.dharker/3749973, accessed 1 March 2024

DIV1.HEADING: First Marriage and Move to India

CHRONSTRUCT.CHRONPROSE: When ID was seventeen, she met her first husband, Indian journalist and writer Anil Dharker, at the University of Glasgow.

SHORTPROSE.P:
He was seven years her senior and was then studying and teaching engineering, though his career was in literary editing. The NationalSurendran, "Obituary", Hindustan Times Following her graduation, the couple eloped to Mumbai (Bombay at the time) where they eventually settled in the upscale residential area of Malabar Hill
BBC, The Conversation The Hindustan Times .
ID was disowned by her Muslim family for her marriage, because Anil Dharker was Hindu. Shamsie 301 The result was a lifetime of estrangement from her parents.
ID has one daughter from her marriage, now the successful actress Ayesha Dharker, who was born in what was thenBombay in 1977.

After spending her entire childhood and adolescence in Glasgow, ID found the move to India was like hanging on to the edge of a cliff by my fingernails, though it exposed her to a wider range of life experiences. to the literary world, and to the nuances of her husband’s Hinduism. She would characterize this time in her life as one where taking risks taught her to be a better writer. Kumar 53Olney Interview, York Notes

P.DIVORCE: ID’s marriage to Dharker ended in 2002.
P.QUOTE: I had married an Indian and gone to Bombay and that was wonderful, and I have a beautiful daughter to show for it. But there was a point where we began to grow apart. Maybe I just grew up. So we had separated.

BIBCIT.PLACEHOLDER: BBC Desert Island Discs, Imtiaz Dharker

SHORTPROSE.P: Anil Dharker died early on 26 March 2021. His obituary in the Hindostan Times called him one of the great liberal Indian editors. Surendran, "Obituary", Hindustan Times

The great love of ID’s life was Simon Powell, the founder of Poetry Live!, an educational organization that presents live poetry readings to schoolchildren studying English: it has been supported by many distinguished British poets, and by 2008 was reaching 100,000 students a year. “Our Founder,” Poetry Live! Immediately after her separation from Anil Dharker, ID received a fax inviting her to read her poetry at forty upcoming events. When I came to read, I met this incredible man called Simon Powell . . . everyone loved him. It took me only a few minutes to realize I was actually in love with him. They were both in their fifties when they married: Powell's first wife had died in November 2002. They had an Indian wedding at which the bridegroom arrived on a white horse. Powell, having lived with cancer for more than a decade, died on 25 October 2009. Greer, Farewell, Guardian November 2010 .
His death was mourned in a poem by Carol Ann Duffy, then Poet Laureate, and a piece in the Guardian by Germaine Greer. Greer, Farewell, Guardian November 2010
ID said later, I never laughed so much in my life as I did with him. We were together seven years, but seventy wouldn’t have been enough. BBC Desert Island Discs Her poetry volume Over the Moon, the first she published after her husband’s death, reflects her grief in many poems. The Markaz Review

DIV1.HEADING: A Multi-Faceted Career: Art and Film

SHORTPROSE.P: In addition to her writing, ID is an accomplished filmmaker and artist. By the time she published her first poetry volume, Purdah, she had held two solo exhibitions of her drawings in what was at the time Bombay. The first shared its title with that of the poetry volume; the second was also seen in Delhi. Three more solo exhibitions followed in Mumbai, and ID continued to exhibit in galleries in New York, London, and Hong Kong as her poetry career took off. Imtiaz Dharker Website, Drawings, http://www.imtiazdharker.com/drawings

In 1980 ID started SOLO, a company for the production, scripting and direction of video films, of which more than 300 have been produced. Imtiaz Dharker Website She has been awarded by the President of India the Silver Lotus for the best short film, and the Balraj Sahni Award by the All India Artists Association.Sonwalkar, "Pakistan-born poet Imtiaz Dharker gets Queen’s Gold Medal", Hindustan Times

ID's filmmaking career had its origins in telling stories about the suffering of children and social justice. Her films for UNICEF, Concern India, and CRY (Child Relief and You) addressed difficult social justice topics including childhood leprosy, unhoused children, and the state of cancer care in India. Newcastle University Imtiaz Dhjarker Website She continues to contribute to causes through her artistic pursuits, and is a committed activist against climate breakdown. In 2015 she contributed to an anthology of poems about climate change, and in 2020 she headlined Verse Aid: Poems for the Earth, hosted by Poets for the Planet (a community of poets, with other artists and activists, committed to raising their voices to engage with the climate emergency). "A climate change poem for today", The Guardian Society of Authors Poets for the Planet

ID and poet Carol Ann Duffy have expressed mutual admiration for each other's work and have shared a friendly professional relationship through many publications, tours, and events. In 2016, Duffy chose ID to join her and three others for Shore To Shore: Celebrating Poetry and Community with the Laureate and Friends, a major national tour of the United Kingdom over fourteen days. Along with Duffy, the other two poets chosen for the tour, Jackie Kay and Gillian Clarke, are ID's good friends and collaborators. Pan Macmillan The Guardian
ID began writing poetry at ten years old, but didn’t realise that it was poetry until a year later, when a teacher included one of her pieces in the school magazine and called it a poem. Bowell

P.TVOICENARRATION: This poem, was about going up in space and although the speaker was looking back at the beautiful earth, he felt homesick. He knew it was a giant step for mankind, but he still wanted his own fireside.

SHORTPROSE.P:
ID has said that her choice of poetry as a genre was was strongly intentional. Poetry uses language in a different way. I think it uses language to say things that go beyond the actual meaning of the words. When it speaks well it can say what is really unsayable.
BBC Desert Island Discs
Her poetry and illustrations are meant to be experienced together. She has discussed how, when she places the pen to paper, she never knows if the work is to become a poem or a drawing. In her mind, there is no differentiation between a word or an illustrative line. When I’m thinking around a theme the drawings work around that theme as well. For me it’s just a different extension of the same things that go on in my mind. BBC Desert Island Discs As her poetry developed, ID’s works became interwoven with both personal experiences and broader political and social themes, enmeshed with the medium itself. Poetry speaks back to the whole messy business of living with everything it has. It speaks with the body, the rush of blood to the heart and the music of being human. The Scotsman
Before the publication of Purdah, her first poetry collection, ID worked as the editor of the poetry section of a magazine in India. Purdah, back cover

CHRONPROSE.PRARITIESFEATURESDECORATIONS: The now-defunct Three Crowns Books imprint of Oxford University Press, which published mainly British Commonwealth authors from South Asia and Africa, issued ID’s first collection of poetry, Purdah And Other Poems, which displayed one of her own illustrations on the cover.

SCHOLARNOTE.P: Purdah, a Hindi word which translates as curtain, is the name given to the practice in some Hindu and Muslim societies of secluding women from public observation through the wearing of the veil and through the use of screens and separate rooms within the home.

SHORTPROSE.P:
In this collection, ID’s poems explore the experience of being between cultures and identities, displacement, borders both public and internal, entrapment and freedom, and the veil which seclud[es] women from public observation. Jenkins 121
They have all been sold and bought, / the girls I knew / unwilling virgins who had been taught to hide their brightness, but especially in this strangers' land. TLS 4921 (8 August 1997), 16 These poems display the tension between veiling and revealing that accompanied ID in her life as she straddled two worlds in being a Glaswegian of South Asian heritage. In the poem Borderlands she navigates the balancing on and the crossing of lines that are simultaneously psychological and geographical, the fine margin between being trapped and being free.
Jenkins 121-2

CHRONPROSE.PEDITIONS: A Bloodaxe edition of ID's Postcards from God followed the first edition, published in New Delhi by Viking in 1994. The Bloodaxe edition (bearing on its cover her stretch of a ramshackle street) added to this collection the poems from her first volume, Purdah.

P.PRELATIONSWITHPUBLISHER: This was her earliest publication in Britain, and began her successful twenty-year partnership with Bloodaxe, who have since published all her poetry volumes.

SHORTPROSE.P: Postcards from God I, the poem she places first among those on her webside, opens: Yes, I do feel like a visitor, / a tourist in this world / that I once made. The volume mounts a more explicit social critique than Purdah, presenting especially people forced to exist in the overcrowded conditions of Mumbai. Dharker's overtly political language serves to turn her rage outward. Subramanium In these poems, anguish at a metropolis ravaged by extremism and fundamentalist intolerance expresses itself in an idiom that is flat, terse and — in contrast to the earlier book — unveiled.img: no bibcit so we don't know whose opinion this is. Since the next vol is also, contradictorily, said to be the first that speaks unveiled, I suggest deleting the recent quote tag, reading "flat and terse" and ending there. The accompanying illustrations were created as visual rhymes for the poems to demonstrate the relationship between words and lines of drawing as one where the drawings are an extension of the words, rather than an illustration of them. Chandran N, 1995 They are designed to slow down the pace of reading, or perhaps to urge us to read the sketches and scan the poems. Chandran N, 1995

P.RRESPONSES:
Not all reviews were entirely favourable. Poet Jerry Pinto asserted that the rage he sensed in ID’s blunt-edged poetry had turned some of the poems into posters, the images into slogans.
Pinto 2006

Writing in India Today, Shama Futehally remarked on Dharker's outward consideration of a world beset by unrelenting violence and poverty, and her technique of writing from the perspective of God to combine grave truths and spoken idiom in her writing. He described the collection as poetry which makes it much easier for us to come to terms with the world around us. He was critical of some poems for failing to follow through on their ambitions, and wrote that some fall victim to diagramming, that is, they rely too heavily on the illustrations to direct the reading and interpretation. Casually brushing off any suggestion of chauvinism, he suggested that Dharker's success in representing details of the world in her poetry is in part a result of her being a woman, though he also perceived her worldview as unique among women artists. India Today
Sen Sudeep in the Times Literary Supplementcommented on ID's tightly wrought Muslim sensibility, one that is further strengthened by her individual Indian woman's point of view. TLS 4921 (8 August 1997), 16

TEXTSCOPE.PLACEHOLDER: Imtiaz Dharker, I Speak for the Devil, 2001

CHRONPROSE.PMATERIALCONDITIONS: ID’s third collection of poetry was published the year before she separated from her husband of almost thirty years.

SHORTPROSE.P: ID's poems in I Speak For the Devil engage deeply with themes of women’s bodies as territories - sites of transition and possession - and of the physical resistance of women to subjection by narrow definitions of identity as prescribed under patriarchy. Where some thought Purdah and Postcards From God speak from behind the veil, ID here pushes her work to new places beyond those restrictions. The starting-point . . . is the strip-tease about what happens when the self squeezes past the easy cage of bone.’” Haider 247 Subramaniam This volume includes the poem Honour Killing, about a young woman named Samia Sarwar who in 1999 was shot dead at a lawyer's office in Lahore by an assassin her mother hired because she had filed for divorce. Das
This poem has attracted high levels of commentary and study notes, and has featured on the British GCSC syllabus.

TEXTSCOPE.PLACEHOLDER: Imtiaz Dharker, The Terrorist at my Table, 2006

CHRONSTRUCT.CHRONPROSE: Bloodaxe Books published ID's next poetry volume, The Terrorist at my Table. Dharker and her already [I]nternationally acclaimed daughter were both present at the launch of its Indian ediion in New Delhi in May 2007.

BIBCIT.PLACEHOLDER: Hindustan Times, An evening with a 'terrorist

P.PEDITIONS: The cover of the English edition features ID's drawing of a female figure whose eyes are all that is visible beneath loose, enveloping, striped garments.

SHORTPROSE.P:
Many poems here are either direct or indirect responses to the 11 September 11 2001 World Trade Center attacks, and explore the complexities of cultural and personal identity in a world marked by conflict and change. questions of identity, belonging, and the impact of global events on individual lives. But How to Cut a Pomegranate (about a fruit that turns up regularly in Dharker's work) deals not with violence (despite its opening: Never, said my father, / Never cut a pomegranate / through the heart. It will weep blood.) but with nostalgia and the loss of imagined beauty, with sensuous images matching the beauty of blood-red pomegranate seeds. The juice tasted of gardens / I had never seen . . . . The pomegranate reminded me / that somewhere I had another home.img: can you find better source than I have? Carving, too, belies the sharpness of its title: We let the moment rise / like birds and planes and angels / to the sky. / Eternity is this. Poems on the Underground, https://poemsontheunderground.org/carving, consulted 6 March 2024
I Need asserts need, or greed, for a succession of luscious foods from various cultures, until the final I need is the shortest: I need you to come back. Carol Ann Duffy, Guardian

Carol Ann Duffy chose I Need from this collection as her contribution to a series run by the Guardian of poems to comfort us in isolation (because of Covid19). Duffy judges ID's work to be full of a deep relish for all the world has to offer – food, travel, colour, love – and a lip-smacking relish for words themselves. Carol Ann Duffy, Guardian .

CHRONSTRUCT.CHRONPROSE: In a new poetry volume entitled Leaving Fingerprints, ID focussed on the condition of being lost or missing, on traces left indelibly behind, on liminal spaces, surveillance, and migration.

P.PRARITIESFEATURESDECORATIONS: ID's cover drawing of an enlarged impression of a whorled fingerprint is reminiscent of the way the female figure is dressed in her cover drawing of The Terrorist at my Table.

SHORTPROSE.P: Many of the poems here are set in Sussex landscapes around Hastings. They deal in beaches, shores and fields, sand and soil, the erasure of moments and the detritus of history.

RECEPTION.RRESPONSES: Writing in the Guardian, Sarah Crown reviewed this volume as well-grounded in identity and a sense of place, and noted how skilfully Dharker draws links the two together. Though she praised the symbolic cohesiveness of the volume, she was critical of the symbolism in the title, finding it too broad and obvious. The title doesn’t so much hint as holler . . . it's easy to see the appeal of the fingerprint – with its suggestions of permanence, immutability, above all of ownership – to a woman in exile, unsure of her place in the world. Crown, Guardian 2009 Crown also found the poems at times inconsistent and overblown, though with moments of excellence and glorious images. Guardian 2009

CHRONSTRUCT.CHRONPROSE: Over the Moon, the first volume that ID had published since the loss of her second husband, Simon Powell, explores her feelings around her bereavement: her intense joy in being married to her great love ,and the intense pain of losing him to cancer.

P.PRARITIESFEATURESDECORATIONS: ID's cover features pomegranates, brilliant white on dark, with a tiny crescent moon in a separate frame above.

P.TSETTINGPLACE: The poems here are set mostly in London.

SHORTPROSE.P: In the year of its publication Over the Moon brought ID the Queen's Medal for Poetry, and was shortlisted for the Ted Hughes Award for New Work in Poetry. Gillian Clarke wrote The loss of a great love makes poems of piercing beauty in her finest book to date.Bloodaxe website, https://www.bloodaxebooks.com/ecs/product/over-the-moon-151 The Poetry Society

TEXTSCOPE.PLACEHOLDER: Imtiaz Dharker, Luck is the Hook, 2018

CHRONSTRUCT.CHRONPROSE: Bloodaxe Books published Luck is the Hook, ID's seventh volume of poetry. Her cover drawing depicts a stylized female figure standing under a spherical fruit-bearing tree under a full moon.

P.PMATERIALCONDITIONS: By now ID had been working as a poet for nearly thirty years. She had become estranged from her family, left the country of her formative years, been married twice and widowed once, given birth to a daughter, and found success as a poet and an influential voice in the South Asian writing community.

SHORTPROSE.P:
In Luck Is The Hook ID draws on her life experiences to explore the role of luck, fate or chance in finding or losing people and places that are loved: a change in the weather, a trick of language, a bomb that misses its mark, six pomegranate seeds eaten by mistake;
This refers to the Greek myth of Persephone or Proserpine, who was carried off to Hades. Before her mother, Demeter or Ceres the goddess of harvest, arrived to rescue her she had eaten six pomegranate seeds; this condemned her to stay in Hades for six months of each year, which created winter on earth. all these events cast long shadows and raise questions about who is recording them, about believing, not believing, wanting to believe.Bloodaxe website Chance produces incongruous juxtapositions like an elephant on the Thames in London during a seventeenth-century frost fair.Stephanie Green, Glasgow Review of Books
In the Glasgow Review of Books, Stephanie Green selected this as her stand-out book of poetry this year, an excellent, deeply moving collection.Stephanie Green, Glasgow Review of Books

CHRONSTRUCT.CHRONPROSE: ID published another volume of poems, Shadow Reader, whose cover drawing shows an indistinct female figure standing beside a column: her shadow stretches down a long flight of steps.

SHORTPROSE.P: ID was among thirty poets selected for the anthology On Shakespeare's Sonnets: A Poets' Celebration, 2016, alongside Carol Ann Duffy and Gilian Clarke, with whom she had previously travelled for nationwide readings in Shore To Shore: Celebrating Poetry and Community TLS Her contribution was one of three which the Times Literary Supplement selected for reprinting.TLS 5888 (5 February 2016): 15

She contributed The Trick, a response to Shakespeare's Sonnet 43 (When most I wink, then do mine eyes best see, a poem of repetition, paradox, and zeugma, about seeing the beloved most clearly when asleep). On Shakepeare's Sonnets, 78
The title, sonnet form and content of her response reflect her description of the way Shakespeare plays tricks on the mind with light and shade, with words that look and sound the same but make cunning grammatical shifts. But hers is also a poem of deep feeling about loss of the beloved except in dreams: For this, for this, I fall asleep in haste, / willing to fall for the trick that tells the truth.
On Shakespeare's Sonnets, 78

P.RRESPONSES: Early Modern scholar Hannah Crawforth, an editor of the anthology, praised Dharker's nuanced appoach to the intimidating task of responding to Shakespeare, writing that The Trick strikes en elegant balance between emulation and innovation, indebtedness and resistance, proximity and distance.

SHORTPROSE.P: ID has been sought after for readings and appearances, and has read her poetry both in the UK and on the international touring circuit. She has recorded poems both for The Poetry Archive and Poetry Archive Scotland, and is well represented in Poetry Foundation.Poetry Archive, https://poetryarchive.org/, accessed 6 March 2024Poetry Foundation, https://www.poetryfoundation.org/, accessed 6 March 2024 In 2002 she was invited by the Wordsworth Trust to read her work alongside thirty other poets working at the cutting edge of their art. TLS In 2011, Dharker joined a group of thirty migrant poets who read and recorded their work for the Arts Council-funded poetry recording project Between Two Worlds: Poetry and Translation, which was conceptualised and produced by Punjabi poet Amarjit Chandan. TLS In 2020 she was one of selectors for Poems on the Underground, and she is one of three editors of the Poems on the Underground website. https://poemsontheunderground.org/about-us

In 2014 ID was awarded the Queen's Gold Medal for Poetry. This significant achievement was also personally important in the context of her upbringing in a strongly monarchist household. When my father died he was almost 100 years old. Occasionally names would slip out of his head. The person he remembered was the Queen; he spoke of her up to the end with great admiration and affection. I know he would be delighted, and would have forgiven me somehow if he knew. BBC Desert Island Discs

P.RRECOGNITIONS: ID is a Fellow of the Royal Society of Literature. She was awarded an honorary doctorate from SOAS, University of in London in 2016, and in 2019 she was one of those seriously considered to succeed Carol Ann Duffy as Poet Laureate. She was rumoured to have been offered this huge honour, and was quoted as declining: I had to weigh the privacy I need to write poems against the demands of a public role. The poems won.

WORKSCITED.SOURCE: Sarah Crown, "Review. Leaving Fingerprints by Imtiaz Dharker", Guardian, 5 December 2009, https://www.theguardian.com/books/2009/dec/05/leaving-fingerprints-imtiaz-dharker-review, accessed 6 March 2024

Imtiaz Dharker, "A climate change poem for today: X by Imtiaz Dharker", The Guardian, 25 May 2015, https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2015/may/25/a-climate-change-poem-for-today-x-by-imtiaz-dharker, accessed 4 March 2024

Carol Ann Duffy, "Poems to get us through: love, loss and an appetite pricked by longing", Guardian, 30 April 2020, https://www.theguardian.com/books/2020/apr/30/poems-to-get-us-through-i-need-by-imtiaz-dharker, accessed 6 March 2024
"An evening with a 'terrorist', Hindustan Times, 14 May 2007, https://www.hindustantimes.com/books/an-evening-with-a-terrorist/story-68bvLrIoUWSHUadIoFF1pN.html, accessed 4 March 2024

Alison Flood, "Hunt for next poet laureate still on as Imtiaz Dharker says no to job", Guardian 3 May 2019, https://www.theguardian.com/books/2019/may/03/hunt-next-poet-laureate-imtiaz-dharker-carol-ann-duffy, accessed 1 March 2024
Stephanie Green, "Reads of the year 2018", Glasgow Review of Books, 4 January 2019, https://www.bloodaxebooks.com/ecs/product/over-the-moon-151, accessed 7 March 2024>
Germaine Greer, Farewell, Guardian November 202, https://www.theguardian.com/books/2010/nov/28/germaine-greer-simon-powell-poetry, accessed 3 March 2024
Poets for the Planet, https://poetsfortheplanet.org.uk/, accessed 4 March 2024
Prasun Sonwalkar, "Pakistan-born poet Imtiaz Dharker gets Queen’s Gold Medal", Hindustan Times (19 December 2014, accessed 2 March 2024
C. P. Surendran, "Obituary: Anil Dharker (1947-2021", Hindustan Times, 26 March 2021
